title: Missing access control on doExchange allows anyone to drain from_bank's USDC balance
claim: The doExchange function is declared public without the onlyOwner modifier, while the contract is trusted by from_bank (verified on-chain). This allows any external caller to drain the from_bank's entire USDC balance through the Curve exchange into to_bank.
impact: Complete loss of all USDC held in the from_bank (0x9Ab872A34139015Da07EE905529a8842a6142971). Funds are irreversibly swapped to USDT and sent to to_bank (0x21A3dbeE594a3419D6037D6D8Cee0B1E10Bf345C) where the attacker may have further extraction paths. The developer clearly intended access control (changeMinimumAmount uses onlyOwner) but forgot it on doExchange.
paths: 1. Attacker identifies that ExchangeBetweenPools (0x765b8d7cd8ff304f796f4b6fb1bcf78698333f6d) is in the from_bank trust list (verified: True)
2. Attacker waits for or causes USDC deposits into from_bank
3. Attacker calls doExchange(amount) with amount up to from_bank's full USDC balance
4. from_bank.issue(address(this), amount) transfers USDC from from_bank to ExchangeBetweenPools
5. ExchangeBetweenPools swaps all USDC to USDT via Curve yPool at 0x45F783CCE6B7FF23B2ab2D70e416cdb7D6055f51
6. USDT is sent to to_bank. The from_bank is drained.

title: Zero slippage protection (min_dy=0) on Curve exchange enables sandwich attacks
claim: The Curve exchange_underlying call hardcodes min_dy=0, providing no slippage protection. Combined with the public doExchange function, an attacker can manipulate the Curve pool price immediately before calling doExchange and reverse it afterward, extracting value from the swap at the expense of from_bank depositors.
impact: An attacker can sandwich-attack every doExchange execution: (1) swap USDC→USDT on Curve to inflate USDT price, (2) trigger doExchange which converts from_bank's USDC at the manipulated bad rate, (3) swap USDT→USDC back at the favorable rate. The attacker profits from the manipulation round-trip while from_bank depositors suffer the loss. The FlawVerifier's Balancer flash loan integration confirms this exploit path is viable.
paths: 1. Attacker flash-loans USDC from Balancer Vault (0xBA12222222228d8ba445958a75a0704d566bf2c8)
2. Attacker swaps a large amount of USDC→USDT on the Curve yPool (0x45F783CCE6B7FF23B2ab2D70e416cdb7D6055f51), pushing up the USDT price
3. Attacker calls doExchange(amount) - the contract swaps from_bank's USDC for USDT at the inflated rate due to min_dy=0, receiving less USDT than fair value
4. Attacker swaps USDT→USDC back on Curve at the favorable inflated rate, profiting from the round-trip
5. Attacker repays Balancer flash loan, keeping the profit
Optimal sandwich amount depends on pool depth; at current yPool depths (~$100M+), manipulation of 1-5% of pool TVL yields measurable profit after fees
